coredata.dc:title: Stable hydrogen bonding interactions in supramolecular deep eutectic solvents based on carbon quantum dots: For extraction and oxidative desulfurization
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Meijun
Zou, Changjun
Tang, Wenyue
Cao, Yixuan
coredata.dc:description: Supramolecular deep eutectic solvent (SUPRADES) was a promising green solvent with physicochemical properties that was similar to those of conventional deep eutectic solvent (DES). The supramolecular deep eutectic solvent-based nanofluidic system (β-CD-CA/CQDs) was constructed by using β-cyclodextrin as the hydrogen bond acceptor and citric acid as the hydrogen bond donor with the addition of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The addition of CQDs enhanced the desulfurization ability and hydrogen bonding stability of SUPRADES. By controlling the content of CQDs solution, the viscosity of β-CD-CA/CQDs can be easily changed without altering its eutectic nature. The effects of temperature, O/S, sulfur content, oil content and different sulfides on the sulfur removal of β-CD-CA/CQDs were investigated. The sulfur removal of β-CD-CA/CQDs was up to 99.32% at 50 °C. The cyclic voltammetric tests, recovery experiments, and characterization all showed that β-CD-CA/CQDs had excellent recoverability and stability in desulfurization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Extractive oxidation desulfurization
Carbon quantum dots
Cyclodextrin
Deep eutectic solvents
Stability

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21576225), Sichuan Province Science and Technology Support Program (2019YFG0054).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of β-CD-CA/CQDs, β-CD-CA, CQDs, β-CD.
1H NMR spectra of CA, β-CD, β-CD-CA, β-CD-CA/CQDs in D2O.
(a)–(c) The TEM image of synthesized β-CD-CA/CQDs. (d) (e) (f) The TEM image of synthesized CQDs.
(a) The 90-day resting period for β-CD-CA/CQDs and β-CD-CA, viscosity of (b) β-CD-CA/CQDs and (c) β-CD-CA/CQDs after dilution using CQDs solution.
(a) Transmission and (b) delta of transmission of β-CD-CA/CQDs.
Effect of reaction temperature on the sulfur removal of (a) β-CD-CA and (b) β-CD-CA/CQDs (experimental conditions: VDES/Voil = 3, S content of 500 ppm, O/S = 3).
Effect of O/S molar ratio on sulfur removal (experimental conditions: S content of 500 ppm, VDES/Voil = 3, T = 30 °C).
Effect of SUPRADES/oil volume ratio on the sulfur removal of β-CD-CA/CQDs (experimental conditions: S content of 500 ppm, O/S = 3, T = 30 °C).
Effect of different sulfur levels on the sulfur removal of β-CD-CA/CQDs (experimental conditions: VDES/Voil = 3, O/S = 3, T = 30 °C).
Effect of different sulfur compounds on the sulfur removal of β-CD-CA/CQDs (experimental conditions: S content of 500 ppm, O/S = 3, VDES/Voil = 3, T = 30 °C,).
(a) Reusability and (b) regeneration performance of β-CD-CA/CQDs (experimental conditions: S content of 500 ppm, O/S = 3, VDES/Voil = 3, T = 30 °C).
The FT-IR spectra of the (a) DBT, the mixture of β-CD-CA/CQDs and DBT and (b) regenerated β-CD-CA/CQDs, used β-CD-CA/CQDs.
(a) 1H NMR spectra of DBT in DMSO, the mixture of β-CD-CA/CQDs and DBT, and (b) used β-CD-CA/CQDs, regenerated β-CD-CA/CQDs in D2O.
(a) Cyclic voltammograms curve tests. (b) The ESR. (c) Possible mechanisms of β-CD-CA/CQDs in EODS.

head.ce:title: Stable hydrogen bonding interactions in supramolecular deep eutectic solvents based on carbon quantum dots: For extraction and oxidative desulfurization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CCDC Drilling Fluid Technology Service CO., LTD, Chengdu 610051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CCDC Drilling Fluid Technology Service CO., LTD

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CCDC Drilling Fluid Technology Service CO., LTD, Chengdu 610051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Southwest Petroleum University, Nanchong 637001, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Southwest Petroleum University, Nanchong 637001, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: No. 8 Xindu Avenue, Xindu District, Chengdu 610500, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supramolecular deep eutectic solvent (SUPRADES) was a promising green solvent with physicochemical properties that was similar to those of conventional deep eutectic solvent (DES). The supramolecular deep eutectic solvent-based nanofluidic system (β-CD-CA/CQDs) was constructed by using β-cyclodextrin as the hydrogen bond acceptor and citric acid as the hydrogen bond donor with the addition of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The addition of CQDs enhanced the desulfurization ability and hydrogen bonding stability of SUPRADES. By controlling the content of CQDs solution, the viscosity of β-CD-CA/CQDs can be easily changed without altering its eutectic nature. The effects of temperature, O/S, sulfur content, oil content and different sulfides on the sulfur removal of β-CD-CA/CQDs were investigated. The sulfur removal of β-CD-CA/CQDs was up to 99.32% at 50 °C. The cyclic voltammetric tests, recovery experiments, and characterization all showed that β-CD-CA/CQDs had excellent recoverability and stability in desulfurization.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also form part of an ongoing study.

ce:section.ce:para: The fossil raw materials are commonly used as fuels nowadays, and the combustion of sulfur compounds in fuels produces different sulfur oxides. This not only causes environmental problems such as fine particulate and acid rain matter, but these also pose a risk to human health, such as respiratory diseases and cancer. Currently, many countries have imposed strict limits on sulfur content in order to reduce environmental pollution [1].

Hydrodesulfurization can easily remove sulfides and is a common desulfurization technology used in industrial production today. Aromatic sulfur compounds in fuels such as 1-benzothiophene (BT), dibenzothiophene (DBT), and especially the derivative 4,6-dimethyldibenzothiophene (4,6-DMDBT) are difficult to remove because of the large spatial resistance. Removal of thiophene derivatives by conventional hydrodesulfurization requires stringent conditions such as higher temperatures and pressures, which not only increases operating costs, but also makes the desulfurization process cumbersome [2]. In order to overcome these drawbacks of hydrodesulfurization, several non-hydrogenated techniques have been developed, such as extractive desulfurization [3,4], oxidative desulfurization [5,6], biological desulfurization [7–9], and adsorption desulfurization [10–12]. In contrast, extractive and oxidative desulfurization (EODS) is the removal of sulfur compounds using a functional solvent with specific dual functions of extraction and oxidation, which allows efficient desulfurization under mild conditions.

DES has similar properties to traditional ionic liquids (ILs), and the synthesis process is simpler and less costly, making it easier to achieve industrial applications [13]. Li et al. [14] were the first to report that DES showed the excellent desulfurization capacity in extractive desulfurization. The commonly prepared DES use choline chloride as the hydrogen bond acceptors (HBA) and a series of hydrogen bond donors (HBD) as the synthetic material [15,16]. And research on DES synthesis led to an interesting discovery: SUPRADESs, all of which currently consist of cyclodextrins (CDs) [17]. Because CDs has the advantages of being naturally degradable and environmentally friendly, and its low price makes it one of the raw materials for DES. The combination of a hydrophilic outer surface with a hydrophobic cavity allows CDs to form inclusion complexes in aqueous solutions of molecules with various hydrophilic and suitable geometries, resulting in non-covalent host–guest interactions [18,19]. The unique structure of CDs allows it to close and fix the spatial geometry between the substrate and the catalytic active site, resulting in selective catalysis and significantly higher reaction rates. Since SUPRADES significantly improves the efficiency of many reaction processes compared to the use of conventional DES, the use of CDs as a component of SUPRADES may prove to be economically viable [20,21]. Tracy et al. [22] first proposed a CDs-based supramolecular solvent to obtain low melting point mixtures by mixing levulinic acid and CDs derivatives. François et al. [23] used β-CD and N, N′-dimethylurea to form DES with high catalytic activity and recoverability. Guan et al. [24] synthesized DES by using CDs as an HBA with organic acid as an HBD, which showed excellent desulfurization ability at room temperature. The viscosity and melting point of CDs as a material for DES are usually very high. DES prepared using CDs and organic acid has excellent adhesion [6]. Nanofluids exhibit unique properties in many ways, and many studies have shown that appropriate nanoparticles can enhance the extraction performance of solvents [25,26]. Several previous studies have shown that nanoparticles can improve the solute's capability to remove sulfur [27,28]. Karolina et al. [29] suggested that the addition of TiO2 and Fe2O3 to ILs can improve the extraction of sulfides from ILs, and the presence of nanoparticles in ILs significantly improved the solute distribution rate and selectivity. In previous studies, nanofluids prepared from carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibited superior stability compared to conventional nanofluids [30]. The viscosity of the DES prepared from CDs and acid itself is higher than that of the conventional solution, which is not conducive to the mass transfer process. According to the references [31–33], the introduction of nanoparticles increases the viscosity of DES, and the increase in viscosity is generally detrimental to the mass transfer process. Therefore, the selection of nanoparticles is particularly important in order to improve the performance of DES. Besides, the storage conditions of DES are also worthy of attention [34]. The addition of water to DES leads to a decrease in viscosity, while causing an increase in its polarity. Although reducing the viscosity of DES enhances mass transfer from the sample to the solution, thus improving the extraction efficiency, increasing the water content weakens the interaction between DES components. In addition, large amounts of water can cause a complete breakdown of DES hydrogen bonds [35]. There is an important factor that hinders the application of DES is the appearance of solid phase. With the formation of the eutectic point, the melting point also decreases gradually, and after cooling for a certain time after full melting, the different components in it produce a solid–liquid multiphase system [34]. For this purpose, a SUPRADES system based on a CQDs solution was designed.

In this work, we prepared CQDs solutions to be added to the novel SUPRADES (β-CD-CA/CQDs) prepared based on CDs and used as both oxidizing agent and catalyst. The effect of the introduction of CQDs on SUPRADES was analyzed. Desulfurization research has found that a variety of factors affect the desulfurization performance of β-CD-CA/CQDs, including reaction temperature, initial sulfur concentration, VDES/Voil, and oxidant dosage. The 1H Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (1H NMR) and Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) were performed on the β-CD-CA/CQD before and after the reaction, and the results showed the strong interaction between DBT and β-CD-CA/CQD. The CQDs and β-CD-CA/CQDs were characterized by using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), and the Electron Spin Resonance (ESR) and Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) of β-CD-CA/CQDs were also measured to investigate the desulfurization mechanism.

Citric acid (CA, 99%), Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, AR, 30%), β-cyclodextrin (β-CD, 99%), and Octane (AR) were supplied by Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory, China. 1-Benzothiophene (BT, 97%) was purchased from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Co., Ltd. Dibenzothiophene (DBT, 97%) was purchased from Shanghai NCC Biotechnology Co., Ltd. 4,6-Dimethyldibenzothiophene (4,6-DMDBT, ≥98%) was purchased from Shanghai Bohr Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All materials are ready for use without further purification.

The 5 g of β-CD was put into 40 mL of distilled water and reacted at 180 °C for 8 h using an autoclave. After obtaining the reddish-brown colloidal solution, it was purified by dialysis membrane for 24 h and used as CQDs solution. β-CD and CA were added to the beaker in a molar ratio of 1:8, and 3 mL of deionized water and CQDs solution were added, respectively. Stir at 90 °C until a clear, homogeneous, and transparent liquid was obtained, noted as β-CD-CA, β-CD-CA/CQDs, respectively (Fig. 1 ).

The Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) was measured using WQF-520. The 1H Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (1H NMR) was measured using BRUKER 400 NMR spectrometer. The morphology of CQDs was measured by TEM (FEI Tecnai F20, USA). The morphology of β-CD-CA/CQDs was measured by Low Voltage Transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Hitachi HT7820, Japan). The densities of β-CD-CA/CQDs and β-CD-CA were measured using a densitometer (Yew Wah of Wings), and the measurements were averaged three times. The viscosity of SUPRADES in the temperature interval 20–50 °C was measured at 200 s−1 used a High Temperature Rheometer (HAAKE, Germany). The Turbiscan Stability Analyzer (TLAB) (Formulaction, France) was used to analyze the stability. The Electron Spin Resonance signal (ESR) is measured by using Bruker EMX-PLUS.

The model oils were prepared by dissolving BT, DBT and 4,6-DMDBT in octane, respectively. Preparation of simulated oil with sulfur concentration of 200–1000 ppm. The 5 mL of simulated oil and different volumes of SUPRADES were heated to the specified temperatures (20 °C, 30 °C, 40 °C, 50 °C) with continuous stirring, and a certain amount of H2O2 (O/S = 3, 5, 7) was added during this process. The upper oil phase was taken out and the sulfur content in the oil phase was measured by the TSN-3000 Fluorescent sulfur and nitrogen tester produced by Jiangsu Jiangfen Electric Instrument Co., Ltd. Use Eq. (1) to calculate the sulfur removal:
(1)
η
( % )
=
C 0
-
C t
C 0
× 100 %
where C0 and Ct are the initial sulfur concentration and the post-reaction sulfur concentration, respectively.

The FT-IR spectra of the materials and raw materials prepared in the research are shown in Fig. 2 . Comparing the FT-IR spectra of β-CD-CA and β-CD, β-CD-CA spectra did not change significantly. In the FT-IR of β-CD at 3421 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of OH and at 1648 cm−1 is the bending vibration of OH. The 1643 cm−1 in CQDs is attributed to the CO bond. The stretching vibration peak of CO in CA at 1718 cm−1 is blue-shifted to 1725 cm−1 in the β-CD-CA spectra, and the addition of CQDs has no effect on β-CD-CA. These results verify the existence of an interaction between CO of CA and OH of β-CD. The variation of these peaks can be used to assess the formation of hydrogen bonds.

To further confirm the formation of hydrogen bonds, the interaction between CA, β-CD, β-CD-CA, and β-CD-CA/CQDs was investigated by 1H NMR as shown in Fig. 3 . After the formation of β-CD-CA, the chemical signals of the H atoms of both β-CD and CA were significantly changed. The disappearance of the 5.07 ppm hydrogen signal in β-CD may be masked by the signal peak of D2O. To further confirm the presence of hydrogen signal, 1H NMR spectra of β-CD-CA with DMSO as solvent were collected (Fig. S1). The presence of hydrogen bonding H-1 in β-CD-CA is verified in Fig. S1, where the addition of CQDs shifts the overall hydrogen bonding of β-CD-CA to the high field, so that the H-1 signal appears at 4.27 ppm in β-CD-CA/CQDs. The spectra of β-CD-CA/CQDs produced two new H signals at 3.12 ppm and 3.98 ppm, indicating the formation of new hydrogen bonds. The addition of CQDs increases the electron cloud density of β-CD-CA and affects the overall hydrogen bonding of β-CD-CA [36].

To observe the dispersion and morphology of CQDs and β-CD-CA/CQDs. As shown in Fig. 4 (a–c), the CQDs are uniformly dispersed in β-CD-CA/CQDs and the morphology is spherical particles. And compared with the TEM of CQDs, a layer of liquid can be seen attached to the CQD surface in β-CD-CA/CQDs. Most of the DES and ILs are denser than conventional organic solvents, and the addition of CQDs also increases its densities, as shown in Table 1 .

The SUPRADES was rested at room temperature for 90 days. β-CD-CA/CQDs did not undergo any decomposition, while β-CD-CA showed decomposition and precipitation of crystal particles as shown in Fig. 5 (a). This is because the functional groups on the surface of CQDs have an impact on the hydrogen bond in β-CD-CA/CQDs [36]. It is clear from the references that there is an important link between the magnitude of viscosity and the sulfur removal [37]. The value of the viscosity of the liquid determines the size of the flow resistance, which in turn depends on the molecular structure of the fluid. From Fig. 5(b), the SUPRADES prepared based on CQDs as solvent shows a stable liquid state between 20 °C and 50 °C, indicating that the solvent presents a liquid state over a wide temperature range. However, the addition of CQDs slightly increased the viscosity of SUPRADES, and it was observed that the viscosity of β-CD-CA/CQDs decreased with the increase of temperature. The viscosity of SUPRADES all showed a stable trend when the temperature was increased to 50 °C. The viscosity of β-CD-CA/CQDs was measured as a function of temperature after dilution with CQDs solution. As shown in Fig. 5(c), the viscosity decreased significantly after diluting the β-CD-CA/CQDs. Moreover, the viscosities of β-CD-CA/CQDs diluted by 20 vol% and 50 vol% were almost the same as the temperature reached 50 °C. The general DES is very viscous, and its viscosity can be reduced by adding pure water, but adding too much water will destroy its hydrogen bonding structure [38]. In contrast, the addition of CQDs solution did not break the hydrogen bond structure of β-CD-CA/CQDs, but enhanced its hydrogen bond strength.

To further investigate the stability of CQDs in β-CD-CA, an instability analysis was performed. The optical probe scans every 30 s at sample height and collects transmitted and backscattered light data at 40 μm height intervals. The blue line indicates the result of the first scan, while the red line represents the result of the last scan, as shown in Fig. 6 . Based on the results of each scan, it can be seen that the transmitted light (T) and average transmitted light intensity (ΔT) values did not change significantly with time and the number of scans, which indicates the excellent stability of CQDs in β-CD-CA and the strong stability structure of β-CD-CA/CQDs.

The reaction temperature is an important factor affecting the extraction reaction. Both the viscosity of DES and the rate of oxidative decomposition of H2O2 were affected by the reaction temperature. The desulfurization performance of SUPRADES at 20–50 °C is shown in Fig. 7 . The sulfur removal of β-CD-CA reached 97.22% at 50 °C, 2.5 h, and the sulfur removal of β-CD-CA/CQDs was 98.01% at 50 °C, 1.5 h. With the increase of temperature, the sulfur removal increases. The low sulfur removal at 20 °C due to the effect of viscosity and active site [39]. Besides, the increase in temperature enhances the Brownian motion of CQDs nanoparticles in β-CD-CA/CQDs, according to the references [25,40] and our previous work [30,26,41], the increase in temperature enhances the Brownian motion of CQDs nanoparticles in β-CD-CA/CQDs, which improves the solution mass transfer capacity and thus accelerates the process of desulfurization. Its desulfurization performance is influenced by many factors such as viscosity, liquid–liquid contact, and nanoparticle interactions.

Generally, two times more H2O2 is required to oxidize DBT to sulfone. As shown in the Fig. 8 , the sulfur removal can reach 99.09% at 2.5 h as O/S increases from 3 to 5 due to the slight decomposition of H2O2 in the desulfurization process by temperature and time. The sulfur removal can reach 99.70% after 3 h at O/S is 7.

The amount of desulfurizer represents the desulfurization capacity and economic feasibility. As shown in the Fig. 9 , the amount of simulated oil used affects the sulfur removal. The sulfur removal decreases with the decrease of β-CD-CA/CQDs to model oil volume ratio. The sulfur removal of more than 98% can be achieved at VDES/Voil at both 1:3 and 1:5. At a VDES/Voil of 1:10, a sulfur removal of 94.24% could be achieved after a desulfurization time of 3 h. At VDES/Voil of 1:15, the sulfur removal was only 90.96% after 3 h. This was due to the limited desulfurization performance of 1 mL of β-CD-CA/CQDs for 15 mL of simulated oil.

The effect of the initial sulfur content in the simulated oil is one of the important indicators to evaluate the capability of the prepared SUPRADES. In this study, the effectiveness of desulfurization was studied with an initial sulfur content of 200–1000 ppm set. As shown in the Fig. 10 , the sulfur removal can reach more than 99% after 2 h at an initial sulfur content of 276 ppm. At the initial sulfur content of 541 ppm and 785 ppm, the sulfur removal could reach 98.50% and 96.23% after 3 h, respectively. And at an initial sulfur content of 993 ppm, the sulfur removal was only 92.89% after 3 h. This indicates that more β-CD-CA/CQDs are required to achieve better desulfurization at higher initial sulfur concentrations.

The organic sulfides in the oil mainly include BT, DBT, and 4,6-DMDBT, etc. The removal rates of different sulfides by β-CD-CA/CQDs were investigated as shown in Fig. 11 . After 3 h, the removal rates of 4,6-DMDBT, DBT and BT were 93.01%, 98.50% and 89.23%, respectively. The desulfurization performance of β-CD-CA/CQDs was in the order of BT < 4,6-DMDBT < DBT. This is mainly influenced by the spatial site resistance effect of sulfur atoms and the electron density. The order of electron density on S atoms of the three thiophene-sulfur compounds is BT (5.739) < DBT (5.758) < 4,6-DMDBT (5.760) [42]. Generally, the higher electron density of the S atom is more likely to oxidize to sulfone. However, due to the presence of a spatial barrier of two methyl groups in 4,6-DMDBT, this prevents the active substance from approaching the S atom [43]. Thus, among the three thiophene sulfur compounds, DBT showed the highest oxidation activity, which is consistent with the experimental result that DBT showed the highest removal rate. This indicates that spatial potential resistance has a slight effect on the desulfurization performance.

The desulfurization performance of β-CD-CA/CQDs recirculation is shown in Fig. 12 . After separating the upper oil phase, a fresh oil phase was added to repeat the desulfurization cycle. In the three cycles, the sulfur removal of β-CD-CA/CQDs reached more than 96%. However, the sulfur removal was only 92.17% after the fourth cycle, which was due to the accumulation of oxidation products that diluted the β-CD-CA/CQDs. Because β-CD-CA/CQDs are soluble in water, while DBTO2 is insoluble in water. The hydrophilic DES can be regenerated by water washing [44,15,45]. Therefore, in order to explore its regeneration performance. The upper oil phase was first collected by decantation, and deionized water was introduced into the β-CD-CA/CQDs phase with constant shaking. The oxidation products were removed by filtration and then regenerated β-CD-CA/CQDs were obtained after evaporation of water under reduced pressure. As shown in Fig. 12 (b), its desulfurization capacity was restored to some extent by regeneration.

The mechanism of β-CD-CA/CQDs desulfurization process is investigated more deeply, the interaction between β-CD-CA/CQDs and DBT, the FT-IR spectra of β-CD-CA/CQDs and DBT and before and after the reaction were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 13 , in the FT-IR spectra of the mixture of β-CD-CA/CQDs and DBT, DBT at 3046 cm−1, 1895 cm−1, 1583 cm−1, 1307 cm−1 the characteristic peaks disappeared, indicating a strong interaction between DBT and β-CD-CA/CQDs, and the aromaticity of DBT was thus reduced, leading to the structural distortion of DBT. To investigate the changes in β-CD-CA/CQDs after the EODS reaction, the β-CD-CA/CQDs did not change before and after the reaction. The characteristic peaks were not significantly shifted, indicating the recyclability of β-CD-CA/CQDs. As shown in the Fig. 13 (b), the regenerated β-CD-CA/CQDs showed the same absorption peaks as the fresh β-CD-CA/CQDs, and this result verified that the structure of β-CD-CA/CQDs was stable during the reaction.

To further investigate the interaction between DBT and β-CD-CA/CQDs, its mixture was investigated using 1H NMR spectroscopy. After adding DBT to β-CD-CA/CQDs, the resonances of all protons of β-CD-CA/CQDs shifted significantly up-field due to the aromatic ring current effect. It is interesting that the H signals in the 1H NMR spectra of β-CD-CA/CQDs after the reaction of the mixture and EODS are comparable. These results again indicate that there is a strong interaction between β-CD-CA/CQDs and DBT. It is well known that the inertness of DBT and its derivatives is mainly attributed to their aromatic nature. Thus, the strong interaction between β-CD-CA/CQDs and DBT weakens the aromaticity of DBT and makes it susceptible to oxidation. The spectrum from Fig. 14 (b) indicates that the structure of the regenerated β-CD-CA/CQDs was not destroyed by the regeneration process.

In order to investigate the redox ability of β-CD-CA and β-CD-CA/CQDs, cyclic voltammograms curve tests were performed. From the CV curves in Fig. 15 (a), it can be observed that β-CD-CA/CQDs have the large current variation interval and produce the strongest current, and the electron migration rate determines the catalytic capacity, this result indicates that β-CD-CA/CQDs have better oxidative catalytic performance. Currently, the main mechanism of using organic acids as catalysts for EODS is that peroxides are generated during the oxidation process [46,47]. To further investigate the mechanism, 5, 5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used as a capture agent to measure the ESR, as shown in Fig. 15(b). The addition of β-CD-CA/CQDs and H2O2, based on the measured ESR signal, indicates that some other radicals were formed during the reaction. That is, the carboxylic acids in β-CD-CA/CQDs are oxidized to peracid by H2O2, the peracid in β-CD-CA/CQD oxidizes the sulfur compound to the corresponding sulfone. and this process is accompanied by the reduction of the peracid. And the reduced peracid can also be oxidized and the next cycle of reaction occurs continuously. The weakening of DBT aromaticity by the interaction between DBT and β-CD-CA/CQDs makes it easier to be oxidized. These results indicate that the designed β-CD-CA/CQDs catalyst can significantly reduce the reaction time.

The addition of CQDs solution not only improved the catalytic ability of β-CD-CA/CQDs, but also improved its hydrogen bonding stability making it easier to store. Moreover, the β-CD-CA/CQDs exhibited a wide and stable eutectic region with variable viscosity. These results by EODS demonstrated that β-CD-CA/CQDs also had the excellent desulfurization capacity at room temperature. It is clear from the CV that β-CD-CA/CQDs had the better oxidative catalytic ability compared to β-CD-CA. The carboxylic acids in β-CD-CA/CQDs can be oxidized by H2O2 to peracid, which can be used as oxidants for DBT at the same time. These results indicated that the CQDs-based SUPRADES prepared in this work had the advantages of controllable viscosity, simple synthesis procedure, easy storage, environmental friendliness, sulfur removal and recoverability, providing a new potential development path for both EODS and SUPRADES.

Meijun Chen: Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Changjun Zou: Funding acquisition, Resources. Wenyue Tang: Validation, Supervision. Yixuan Cao: Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21576225), Sichuan Province Science and Technology Support Program (2019YFG0054).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2023.124491.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary material to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Deep oxidative desulfurization of dibenzothiophene by novel POM-based IL immobilized on well-ordered KIT-6
